It happens very quickly. So, how can you make a good one? First, smile. A genuine smile makes you look friendly and approachable. Second, make eye contact. It shows that you are confident and paying attention. Third, have good posture. Stand up straight and don't slouch. It makes you look more self-assured. Finally, be a good listener. Ask questions and show interest in the other person. People appreciate it when you listen to them. These simple things can help you start any new relationship on a positive note.

questions.text: What is a "first impression"?
choices: The last thing you remember about someone
A detailed report about a person
The feeling someone forms about you when they first meet you
A test you have to pass to make a friend
A gift you give to a new person
questions.explanation: 누군가를 처음 만났을 때 그 사람에 대해 형성되는 느낌이나 의견이라고("the feeling or opinion someone forms about you when they meet you for the first time") 정의됨

questions.text: Which is NOT a tip for making a good first impression mentioned in the passage?
questions.explanation: 미소, 눈 맞춤, 좋은 자세, 경청은 언급되었지만, 자기 자신에 대해 많이 이야기하라는 팁은 없음. 오히려 경청하라고 조언함

questions.text: 위 글에서 좋은 자세를 취하는 것이 왜 중요하다고 말하는가?
choices: 다른 사람을 존중하는 것처럼 보이기 때문에
옷이 더 잘 어울려 보이기 때문에
questions.explanation: 바른 자세는 당신을 더 자신감 있어 보이게 만든다고("makes you look more self-assured") 함

passages.title: The Trojan Horse
passages.text: The story of the Trojan Horse comes from the ancient Greek legend of the Trojan War. The Greeks had been fighting the city of Troy for ten years, but they could not break through its strong walls. So, the clever Greek hero Odysseus came up with a plan. The Greeks built a giant wooden horse and left it outside the gates of Troy. The Trojans thought it was a victory gift from the gods and pulled it into their city. They celebrated all night. But the horse was not empty. It was filled with Greek soldiers. That night, while the Trojans were asleep, the soldiers climbed out, opened the city gates for the rest of the Greek army, and conquered the city. The story is a famous symbol of using cleverness to defeat an enemy.

questions.text: What was the Trojan Horse?
choices: A real horse that could talk
A gift from the Trojans to the Greeks
A giant wooden horse filled with Greek soldiers
A statue of the Greek hero Odysseus
questions.explanation: 그리스 병사들로 가득 찬 거대한 목마라고 설명됨

questions.text: Why did the Greeks build the Trojan Horse?
choices: To show their respect for the Trojans
To trick the Trojans and get inside their city
To use it as a boat to go home
To offer it as a sacrifice to the gods
To use it as a watchtower
questions.explanation: 트로이인들을 속여 성 안으로 들어가기 위한 계략이었음

questions.text: 위 글의 내용과 일치하지 않는 것은?
choices: 그리스 병사들은 밤에 목마에서 나와 성문을 열었다.
이 이야기는 지혜를 사용해 적을 이기는 것의 상징이다.
questions.explanation: 목마 안에는 그리스 병사들이 숨어 있었다고("filled with Greek soldiers") 했으므로, 아무도 없었다는 것은 틀린 설명

passages.title: The Importance of Water
passages.text: Water is essential for all life on Earth. Our bodies are about 60% water. We need water to digest food, carry nutrients, and control our body temperature. You can survive for weeks without food, but only a few days without water. However, many people take water for granted. We use it for drinking, cooking, cleaning, and growing food. But fresh, clean water is a limited resource. In some parts of the world, people suffer from water shortages. We must learn to conserve water. Simple actions like turning off the tap while brushing your teeth, taking shorter showers, and fixing leaky faucets can save a lot of water. Every drop counts.

questions.text: What is the main message of the passage?
choices: Water is a beautiful part of nature.
Our bodies are mostly made of water.
Water is essential, and we must conserve it.
Many people in the world have enough water.
questions.explanation: 물의 중요성을 설명하고, 우리가 물을 아껴 써야 한다고 주장하고 있음

questions.text: Which is NOT a way to conserve water mentioned in the passage?
choices: Turning off the tap while brushing teeth
Drinking bottled water instead of tap water
questions.explanation: 짧은 샤워, 양치할 때 물 잠그기, 새는 수도꼭지 고치기는 언급되었지만, 병에 든 물을 마시라는 내용은 없음

questions.text: 위 글에 따르면, 우리 몸에서 물이 하는 역할이 아닌 것은?
questions.explanation: 음식 소화, 영양분 운반, 체온 조절은 언급되었지만, 뼈를 튼튼하게 한다는 내용은 없음

passages.title: A School Talent Show
passages.text: Our school held a talent show last Friday. Many students showed off their unique talents. There were singers, dancers, magicians, and even a comedian. I decided to participate with my band. We have been practicing a rock song for months. When it was our turn to go on stage, my heart was pounding. The lights were so bright, and I could see hundreds of faces in the audience. I was the lead singer. I took a deep breath and started to sing. At first, I was nervous, but then I saw my friends cheering for me. I started to enjoy the moment. We played our song with all our energy. The audience clapped along to the music. Even though we didn't win the first prize, it was an amazing experience. The most important thing was sharing our passion with others.

questions.text: What did the writer do in the talent show?
questions.explanation: 밴드에서 리드 싱어로서 노래를 불렀다고("I was the lead singer") 나옴

questions.text: How did the writer feel before going on stage?
questions.explanation: 무대에 오르기 전 심장이 두근거렸다고("my heart was pounding") 했으므로 긴장되고 흥분된 상태였음

questions.text: 이 글을 통해 글쓴이가 가장 중요하게 생각하는 것은 무엇인가?
choices: 다른 사람들보다 더 뛰어난 재능을 보여주는 것
자신의 열정을 다른 사람들과 나누는 것
questions.explanation: 글의 마지막에 "The most important thing was sharing our passion with others"라고 명시됨

passages.title: The Amazing Octopus
passages.text: The octopus is one of the most intelligent and fascinating creatures in the ocean. It has a soft body and eight long arms, called tentacles. Each tentacle is covered with suckers that can taste and touch. One of the octopus's most amazing abilities is its skill at camouflage. It can change its skin color and texture in an instant to blend in with its surroundings, like rocks or coral. This helps it hide from predators and ambush its prey. An octopus also has three hearts and blue blood. If it is in danger, it can squirt a cloud of black ink to confuse its enemy and make a quick escape. It is a true master of disguise and survival.

questions.text: What is one of the octopus's most amazing abilities?
choices: Changing its skin color and texture
questions.explanation: 문어의 가장 놀라운 능력 중 하나는 피부색과 질감을 바꾸는 위장술이라고나옴

questions.text: What does an octopus do when it is in danger?
choices: It squirts a cloud of black ink.
It bites its enemy with sharp teeth.
It swims faster than any other creature.
questions.explanation: 위험에 처하면 먹물 구름을 내뿜어 적을 혼란시킨다고 나옴

questions.text: 위 글의 내용과 일치하지 않는 것은?
choices: 문어는 주변 환경에 맞춰 피부색을 바꿀 수 있다.
문어는 심장이 한 개이고 피는 붉은색이다.
문어의 팔에는 맛을 보고 만질 수 있는 빨판이 있다.
questions.explanation: 문어는 심장이 세 개이고 피는 파란색이라고 했으므로, 심장이 한 개이고 피가 붉은색이라는 설명은 틀림

passages.title: A Pen Pal from Another Country
passages.text: My English teacher suggested that we find a pen pal to practice our English. A pen pal is someone you write letters to regularly, often someone who lives far away. I was matched with a boy named Leo from Australia. I wrote my first letter, telling him about my school, my family, and my hobbies. A few weeks later, I received a letter back from him. He told me about his life in Sydney. He loves surfing and has a pet koala living in his backyard (just kidding, he said koalas are wild animals). We have been writing to each other for six months now. We share photos and small gifts. It's so interesting to learn about a different culture and life. Having a pen pal has not only improved my English but also given me a new friend from the other side of the world.

questions.text: What is a pen pal?
choices: A friend who lives next door
A person you write letters to regularly
A type of pen used for writing letters
questions.explanation: 멀리 사는 사람과 정기적으로 편지를 주고받는 친구라고 설명됨

questions.text: Where is the writer's pen pal from?
questions.explanation: 시드니에 사는 호주 소년 레오라고 나옴

questions.text: 펜팔을 통해 글쓴이가 얻은 것이 아닌 것은?
choices: 호주로 여행 갈 수 있는 기회
questions.explanation: 영어 실력 향상, 문화 이해, 새로운 친구를 얻는 것 등은 언급되었지만, 호주 여행 기회를 얻었다는 내용은 없음

passages.title: The Invention of the Telephone
passages.text: Before the telephone was invented, long-distance communication was slow. People had to send letters, which could take weeks or months to arrive. Or they used the telegraph, which could only send messages in code. This all changed thanks to an inventor named Alexander Graham Bell. Bell was working on a way to send human voice over wires. On March 10, 1876, he made the first successful telephone call. He was in one room, and his assistant, Thomas Watson, was in another. Bell spoke into the device, "Mr. Watson, come here. I want to see you." Watson heard the words clearly. This invention completely changed the world. It made communication faster and brought people closer together.

questions.text: Who is credited with inventing the telephone?
questions.explanation: 알렉산더 그레이엄 벨이 전화기를 발명한 것으로 알려져 있음

questions.text: What were the first words spoken on the telephone?
choices: "Mr. Watson, come here. I want to see you."
questions.explanation: 최초의 통화 내용은 "Mr. Watson, come here. I want to see you."였음

questions.text: 전화기가 발명되기 전, 사람들이 장거리 소통을 위해 사용했던 방법은?
questions.explanation: 전화기 발명 전에는 편지나 전보를 사용했다고 나옴

passages.title: A Day at the Amusement Park
passages.text: Last Saturday, my friends and I went to an amusement park. I love the thrill of roller coasters. The first ride we went on was the "Mega Drop." It takes you high up into the sky and then suddenly drops you. I screamed my head off, but it was so much fun. After that, we rode a looping roller coaster and got dizzy. We also enjoyed some classic rides like the Ferris wheel and the bumper cars. From the top of the Ferris wheel, we could see the entire park. It was a beautiful sight. We ate cotton candy and popcorn. The day went by so fast. We left the park feeling tired but extremely happy. It was a perfect day to forget about school and just have fun.

questions.text: What is the writer's favorite thing about amusement parks?
questions.explanation: 글의 첫 문장에 "I love the thrill of roller coasters"라고 명시됨

questions.text: Which ride gave the writer a beautiful view of the entire park?
questions.explanation: 대관람차 꼭대기에서 공원 전체를 볼 수 있었다고 나옴

questions.text: 위 글에 나타난 글쓴이의 기분으로 가장 적절한 것은?
questions.explanation: '스릴', '너무 재미있었다', '극도로 행복했다' 등의 표현을 통해 신나고 행복한 기분임을 알 수 있음

passages.title: The Problem of Plastic Pollution
passages.text: Plastic is a very useful material. It's cheap, strong, and can be made into almost anything. Look around you. You will see plastic in your phone, your clothes, and your water bottle. However, our love for plastic has created a huge problem: pollution. Most plastic is not biodegradable, which means it does not break down naturally. It stays in the environment for hundreds of years. Plastic waste ends up in our oceans, harming marine animals. They can mistake plastic bags for food or get tangled in plastic rings. We need to reduce our use of plastic. We can use reusable bags, water bottles, and coffee cups. We must also recycle plastic properly. Every small action helps protect our planet.

questions.text: What is the main problem with plastic mentioned in the passage?
choices: It is not biodegradable and causes pollution.
There is not enough plastic in the world.
questions.explanation: 대부분의 플라스틱이 자연 분해되지 않아 환경 오염을 일으키는 것이 큰 문제라고 설명함

questions.text: How does plastic waste harm marine animals?
choices: They like the taste of plastic.
It makes the ocean water warmer.
They can mistake it for food or get tangled in it.
It makes it hard for them to see.
questions.explanation: 해양 동물들이 플라스틱을 먹이로 착각하거나 플라스틱에 몸이 얽힐 수 있다고("mistake plastic bags for food or get tangled") 나옴

questions.text: 이 글에서 플라스틱 사용을 줄이기 위한 방법으로 제안되지 않은 것은?
questions.explanation: 재사용 가능한 제품 사용, 재활용 등은 제안되었지만, 플라스틱 제품을 전혀 사용하지 말라는 극단적인 제안은 없음

passages.title: A Total Mess
passages.text: My parents went on a trip for the weekend, leaving me home alone for the first time. I was excited. I invited my best friend, Mark, over. We decided to bake a cake. But we are not good bakers. We spilled flour all over the kitchen floor. We cracked an egg, and it dripped down the side of the counter. The cake itself got burnt in the oven and filled the house with smoke. The kitchen was a total mess. We looked at each other and just started laughing. It was a disaster, but a funny one. We spent the next two hours cleaning everything up. We didn't get to eat any cake, but we definitely learned a lesson: follow the recipe carefully!

questions.text: What did the writer and his friend try to do?
questions.explanation: 친구와 함께 케이크를 구우려고("decided to bake a cake") 했다고 나옴

questions.text: Which of the following did NOT happen in the kitchen?
choices: Flour was spilled on the floor.
An egg dripped down the counter.
The house was filled with smoke.
questions.explanation: 밀가루를 쏟고, 계란을 흘리고, 케이크를 태우는 등 여러 사건이 있었지만, 화재경보기가 울렸다는 내용은 언급되지 않음

questions.text: 이 경험을 통해 글쓴이가 배운 교훈은 무엇인가?
choices: 부모님 없이는 아무것도 할 수 없다.
questions.explanation: 글의 마지막에 "follow the recipe carefully!"라는 교훈을 배웠다고 명시됨

passages.title: The Five Senses
passages.text: We experience the world through our five senses: sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Our eyes give us the sense of sight, allowing us to see colors, shapes, and movements. Our ears give us the sense of hearing, letting us hear music, voices, and all the sounds around us. Our nose gives us the sense of smell, which can warn us of danger (like smoke) or bring us pleasure (like the smell of flowers). Our tongue gives us the sense of taste, so we can enjoy different flavors like sweet, sour, salty, and bitter. Our skin gives us the sense of touch, allowing us to feel textures like soft or rough, and temperatures like hot or cold. These five senses work together to give us a complete picture of the world.

questions.text: What is the main topic of the passage?
choices: The five senses and their functions
The importance of the sense of sight
The different flavors we can taste
questions.explanation: 시각, 청각, 후각, 미각, 촉각이라는 다섯 가지 감각과 각각의 기능에 대해 설명하고 있음

questions.text: Which sense can warn us of danger like smoke?
questions.explanation: 연기 냄새처럼 후각이 위험을 경고해 줄 수 있다고("smell, which can warn us of danger (like smoke)") 나옴

questions.text: 위 글의 내용과 일치하는 것을 고르시오.
choices: 우리는 세상을 여섯 가지 감각으로 경험한다.
다섯 가지 감각은 각각 독립적으로 작용한다.
questions.explanation: 피부가 우리에게 촉각을 느끼게 해준다고("Our skin gives us the sense of touch") 명시됨

passages.title: The Art of Mime
passages.text: A mime is a type of performer who tells a story without using words. They use body movements, gestures, and facial expressions to communicate their ideas and emotions. A famous mime act is pretending to be trapped in an invisible box. The mime uses their hands to show the walls of the box, creating the illusion for the audience. Mimes often wear white face paint to make their facial expressions clearer. They also typically wear black and white clothing to help the audience focus on their movements. Becoming a mime requires great physical control and imagination. It is a silent art form that can be both funny and touching.

questions.text: How does a mime tell a story?
choices: By using body movements and facial expressions
questions.explanation: 말을 사용하지 않고 몸짓과 얼굴 표정으로 이야기를 전달한다고 나옴

questions.text: Why do mimes often wear white face paint?
choices: To make their facial expressions clearer
Because it is a tradition with no reason
To protect their skin from the sun
questions.explanation: 얼굴 표정을 더 명확하게 보이게 하기 위해 흰색 분장을 한다고 함

questions.text: 위 글의 내용과 일치하지 않는 것은?
choices: 마임은 말을 사용하지 않고 이야기를 전달하는 공연자이다.
보이지 않는 상자 안에 갇힌 척하는 것은 유명한 마임 연기이다.
마임 배우가 되려면 뛰어난 신체 조절 능력이 필요하다.
마임은 항상 재미있기만 한 예술 형식이다.
마임 배우는 보통 흑백 의상을 입는다.
questions.explanation: 재미있을 수도 있고 감동적일 수도 있다고 했으므로, 항상 재미있기만 하다는 것은 틀린 설명

passages.title: The Greenhouse Effect
passages.text: The greenhouse effect is a natural process that warms the Earth's surface. When the sun's energy reaches the Earth, some of it is reflected back into space, and the rest is absorbed by the land and oceans. This absorbed energy is then released back as heat. Certain gases in the atmosphere, like carbon dioxide, trap some of this heat. They act like the glass walls of a greenhouse, keeping the Earth warm enough for us to live. This is the natural greenhouse effect, and it's a good thing. However, human activities, like burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, and gas), are releasing too much carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. This is making the greenhouse effect stronger and causing the Earth's temperature to rise. This is often called global warming or climate change.

questions.text: What is the natural greenhouse effect?
choices: A process that cools the Earth
A process where gases trap heat to keep the Earth warm
A type of house made of glass
A problem caused by too much rain
questions.explanation: 대기 중의 가스들이 열을 가두어 지구를 따뜻하게 유지하는 자연적인 과정이라고 설명됨

questions.text: How are humans making the greenhouse effect stronger?
choices: By releasing too much carbon dioxide from burning fossil fuels
By reflecting more sunlight back to space
questions.explanation: 인간이 화석 연료를 태워 너무 많은 이산화탄소를 배출함으로써 온실 효과를 강화시킨다고 나옴

questions.text: 위 글에 따르면, '온실 효과'라는 이름이 붙은 이유는 무엇인가?
choices: 대기 중의 가스들이 온실의 유리벽처럼 열을 가두기 때문에
주로 온실 작물에 영향을 미치기 때문에
questions.explanation: 대기 중 가스들이 온실의 유리벽처럼 열을 가두는 역할을 하기 때문임

passages.title: A School Field Day
passages.text: Our school's field day was last Friday. It wasn't a typical sports day with races and competitions. Instead, the theme was "Cooperation and Fun." We were divided into mixed-grade teams. The first game was called "Human Knot." Our team had to stand in a circle, grab hands with two different people across from us, and then try to untangle ourselves without letting go. It was chaotic but hilarious. Another game was "Pass the Water," where we had to pass a cup of water over our heads using only our feet. We got completely wet but laughed so hard. There were no winners or losers, just teams working together and having a good time. It was a great way to build teamwork and make new friends from different grades.

passages.title: The Legend of Robin Hood
passages.text: Robin Hood is a legendary hero from English folklore. The stories say he lived in Sherwood Forest with his group of followers, known as the "Merry Men." Robin Hood was an expert archer. He was famous for "robbing from the rich and giving to the poor." At that time, England was ruled by the cruel Prince John and his greedy sheriff of Nottingham. They taxed the poor people heavily. Robin Hood fought against this injustice. He was an outlaw, but he was a hero to the common people. Whether Robin Hood was a real historical figure or just a legend is still debated. But his story of fighting for justice and helping the needy has been popular for centuries.

choices: Robbing from the rich and giving to the poor

passages.title: The Simple Joy of Baking Bread
passages.text: There is something magical about baking your own bread. It starts with just four simple ingredients: flour, water, yeast, and salt. The process requires patience. You mix the ingredients to form a sticky dough. Then comes the most important part: kneading. You push, pull, and fold the dough until it becomes smooth and elastic. This develops the gluten, which gives the bread its structure. Then, you let the dough rest in a warm place. The yeast works its magic, making the dough rise to double its size. Finally, you bake it. The smell of freshly baked bread that fills the house is one of the best smells in the world. Taking a bite of a warm slice of bread that you made with your own hands is a simple but profound joy.

passages.title: The International Space Station (ISS)
passages.text: The International Space Station (ISS) is a large spacecraft that orbits the Earth. It is a home and a science laboratory for astronauts from many different countries. The ISS was built as a collaborative project among five space agencies: from the USA, Russia, Japan, Europe, and Canada. It is a symbol of international cooperation. Life on the ISS is very different from life on Earth. Because of microgravity, astronauts float instead of walk. They have to strap themselves down to sleep. Eating is also tricky; food has to be specially packaged. Astronauts on the ISS conduct important experiments in fields like biology, physics, and medicine. What they learn helps us understand more about space and can also have benefits for us here on Earth.

passages.title: The Power of Positive Thinking
passages.text: Do you see the glass as half full or half empty? This question is often used to describe two different ways of thinking: optimism and pessimism. Optimists, who see the glass as half full, tend to focus on the good side of things. Pessimists, who see it as half empty, tend to focus on the bad side. Research shows that positive thinking can have a real impact on your life. Optimists are often healthier, less stressed, and more successful in achieving their goals. This is because a positive attitude helps you to be more resilient when you face challenges. It doesn't mean you should ignore problems. It means you should approach them with a hopeful and proactive mindset. Believing in yourself and your ability to succeed is the first step towards success.

questions.text: What does it mean to see a glass as "half full"?
choices: To focus on the positive side of things

questions.text: According to the passage, what is a benefit of being an optimist?

passages.title: A World Without Bees
passages.text: Bees are small insects, but they play a huge role in our ecosystem. They are one of the most important pollinators. A pollinator is an animal that moves pollen from one plant to another, which helps the plants produce fruit and seeds. About one-third of the food we eat, including fruits like apples and strawberries, and vegetables like broccoli and cucumbers, depends on pollination by bees. Unfortunately, the world's bee population is in decline. This is due to problems like habitat loss, pesticide use, and climate change. A world without bees would mean a world with much less food. It would be a disaster for both nature and humans. We need to protect these tiny, hardworking creatures.

questions.text: What is a "pollinator"?
choices: An animal that moves pollen between plants
questions.explanation: 한 식물에서 다른 식물로 꽃가루를 옮기는 동물이라고 정의됨

They are a symbol of hard work.

passages.title: The Day I Got Lost
passages.text: When I was eight, my family went to a large, crowded department store. I was holding my mom's hand, but I let go for just a second to look at a toy. When I looked back, she was gone. I looked around, but I couldn't see her anywhere. Panic set in. The store seemed huge and full of strangers. I started to cry. A kind store employee saw me. She knelt down and asked for my name and my mom's name. She spoke in a calm and gentle voice, which made me feel a little better. She took me to the customer service center and made an announcement over the speakers. A few minutes later, my mom came running. She hugged me so tightly. That day, I learned how important it is to stay close to my parents in a crowded place.

questions.text: Why did the writer get lost?
choices: He ran away from his mom.
He let go of his mom's hand to look at a toy.
He fell asleep in the store.
He went to the wrong floor.

passages.title: The Art of Listening
passages.text: We often think that being a good conversationalist means being a good talker. But being a good listener is just as, if not more, important. True listening is not just waiting for your turn to speak. It's an active process. It means paying full attention to the other person. You should make eye contact and nod to show you are following along. Try to understand not just the words they are saying, but also the feelings behind them. Don't interrupt or start thinking about your reply while they are still talking. When you listen well, you make the other person feel respected and understood. This strengthens your relationships and helps you learn new things. It is a skill that can be improved with practice.

questions.text: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Being a good talker is the most important skill.
The importance and techniques of being a good listener.

passages.title: The Magic of Music
passages.text: Music has a special power that can touch our hearts and souls. It can make us feel happy when we are sad, or calm when we are anxious. Music exists in every culture around the world, though it may sound very different from place to place. Scientists have discovered that listening to music can actually change our brain chemistry. It can release chemicals called endorphins, which make us feel good. Music can also help us remember things better. That's why many children learn the alphabet through songs. Playing a musical instrument is even more beneficial. It improves coordination between our hands and brain. It also teaches discipline and patience. Whether you are listening to classical music, pop songs, or traditional folk music, you are experiencing one of humanity's greatest gifts.

passages.title: The Story of Pizza
passages.text: Pizza, now one of the world's most popular foods, has an interesting history. It started in Naples, Italy, in the 18th century. Poor people in Naples needed cheap, portable food that they could eat with their hands. They began putting tomatoes, oil, and garlic on flat bread. This was the first pizza. In 1889, a pizza maker named Raffaele Esposito created a special pizza for Queen Margherita of Italy. He used tomatoes, mozzarella cheese, and basil to represent the colors of the Italian flag: red, white, and green. This became known as Pizza Margherita. Italian immigrants brought pizza to America in the late 1800s. At first, only Italian Americans ate it. But after World War II, American soldiers who had been in Italy brought back a love for pizza. Soon, pizza became popular all over America and then the world.

passages.title: My Grandmother's Garden
passages.text: Every summer, I visit my grandmother who lives in the countryside. She has a beautiful garden behind her house. In her garden, she grows vegetables like tomatoes, carrots, and lettuce, and fruits like strawberries and apples. But my favorite part of her garden is the flower section. She has roses of different colors, sunflowers that grow taller than me, and lavender that smells wonderful. My grandmother taught me how to plant seeds and water them properly. She showed me how to recognize when vegetables are ready to be picked. We would wake up early in the morning to work in the garden together. She always said, "A garden teaches you patience and rewards you with beauty and food." Working in her garden has made me appreciate where our food comes from and how much care it takes to grow it.

passages.title: The Mystery of Dreams
passages.text: Every night when you sleep, you enter a fascinating world: the world of dreams. Dreams are sequences of images, emotions, and thoughts that happen in your mind while you sleep. Scientists still don't fully understand why we dream, but they have some interesting theories. One theory suggests that dreams help our brain process and organize the information we learned during the day. Another theory says that dreams help us practice dealing with difficult situations. Most dreams occur during a sleep stage called REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep. During this time, your eyes move quickly, but your body is paralyzed to prevent you from acting out your dreams. Some people remember their dreams clearly, while others forget them as soon as they wake up. Dreams can be happy, scary, strange, or realistic. They remain one of the great mysteries of the human mind.

questions.text: What are dreams?
choices: Real events that happen while we sleep
Sequences of images, emotions, and thoughts during sleep
Stories we create when we're awake

passages.title: The Helpful Robot
passages.text: Last week, our science teacher brought a small robot to class. It was about the size of a basketball and had wheels instead of legs. The robot's name was Helper, and it could do many amazing things. It could navigate around the classroom without bumping into desks or students. It had cameras for eyes and could recognize faces. When the teacher asked it to find someone, it would roll over to that person and say hello. Helper could also carry light objects like books or pencils. The most impressive thing was that it could answer simple questions about science. When a student asked, "What is the largest planet?" Helper replied, "Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system." The teacher explained that robots like Helper are becoming more common in hospitals, homes, and offices. They can help people with various tasks and make life easier.

passages.title: The Importance of Recycling
passages.text: Recycling is the process of turning used materials into new products. It's one of the most important things we can do to protect our environment. When we recycle, we reduce the amount of waste that goes to landfills. We also save natural resources like trees, water, and minerals. For example, recycling one ton of paper can save 17 trees, 7,000 gallons of water, and enough energy to power a home for six months. There are many things you can recycle: paper, plastic bottles, glass jars, aluminum cans, and old electronics. The recycling process usually involves collecting, sorting, and processing the materials. Then they are made into new products. However, recycling is only effective if people participate. We all need to do our part by separating our trash and putting recyclable items in the right bins.

passages.title: A Message from the Past
passages.text: While cleaning out my grandfather's attic, I found an old wooden chest. Inside, there were yellowed photographs, old coins, and a bundle of letters tied with a faded ribbon. The letters were written in beautiful handwriting that looked very different from today's writing. As I read them, I realized they were love letters between my grandfather and grandmother from the 1950s. In the letters, my grandfather wrote about his daily life, his hopes for the future, and his deep love for my grandmother. He described going to the movies, dancing at local halls, and saving money to buy her an engagement ring. My grandmother's letters were equally touching. She wrote about waiting for his letters and how happy they made her feel. Reading these letters was like stepping back in time. It made me realize how precious written words can be and how they can preserve our most important memories.

passages.title: The Amazing Journey of Monarch Butterflies
passages.text: Every year, millions of monarch butterflies make one of nature's most incredible journeys. These delicate insects travel up to 3,000 miles from Canada and the United States to central Mexico. What makes this journey even more amazing is that the butterflies that start the trip are not the same ones that finish it. The journey takes multiple generations. The butterflies that begin the trip in the fall will mate and die along the way. Their children and grandchildren continue the journey. Even more mysterious is how they know where to go. Scientists believe they use the sun, magnetic fields, and even genetic memory to navigate. The butterflies that reach Mexico will spend the winter there, clustered together on trees to stay warm. In the spring, they begin the journey back north, but again, it takes several generations to complete the round trip.

passages.title: The School Chess Club
passages.text: I joined the school chess club three months ago, and it has been an amazing experience. Chess is often called "the game of kings" because it requires strategy, patience, and deep thinking. At first, I only knew the basic moves of each piece. The bishop moves diagonally, the rook moves in straight lines, and the knight moves in an L-shape. Our club teacher, Mr. Kim, taught us advanced strategies like controlling the center of the board and protecting our king. He also taught us famous openings like the "Queen's Gambit" and the "Sicilian Defense." What I love most about chess is that every game is different. You have to think several moves ahead and try to predict what your opponent will do. Playing chess has improved my concentration and problem-solving skills. It has also taught me that sometimes you have to sacrifice something small to gain something bigger.

Because the king is the most important piece

passages.title: The Science of Rainbows
passages.text: A rainbow is one of nature's most beautiful phenomena. But do you know how rainbows form? It all starts with sunlight, which looks white but actually contains all the colors of the spectrum: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet. When sunlight passes through water droplets in the air, something magical happens. The water acts like a tiny prism, splitting the white light into its component colors. This process is called refraction. For you to see a rainbow, the sun must be behind you and there must be water droplets in front of you. That's why rainbows often appear after it rains, when the sun comes out while there are still water droplets in the air. The colors always appear in the same order, with red on the outside and violet on the inside. Sometimes you can see a double rainbow, which happens when light is reflected twice inside the water droplets.

choices: Sunlight passing through water droplets and splitting into colors

passages.title: The Art of Origami
passages.text: Origami, the Japanese art of paper folding, is much more than just a hobby. The word "origami" comes from two Japanese words: "ori" meaning "to fold" and "kami" meaning "paper." This ancient art form requires only one material: a square piece of paper. No scissors, glue, or tape are allowed. Despite this limitation, origami artists can create incredibly complex and beautiful sculptures. From simple cranes and flowers to detailed dragons and geometric shapes, the possibilities are endless. Origami teaches important life skills. It requires precision, patience, and the ability to follow detailed instructions. It also helps develop spatial reasoning and fine motor skills. Many schools now use origami in math classes because it helps students understand geometry and fractions. The meditative nature of folding paper can also reduce stress and improve focus. What starts as a flat piece of paper becomes a three-dimensional work of art through the magic of folding.

passages.title: A Day at the Science Museum
passages.text: Last Saturday, my family visited the city science museum. It was much more exciting than I had expected. The museum had interactive exhibits where we could touch and experiment with different scientific concepts. In the physics section, I played with a giant pendulum and learned how it always swings back to the same height. The chemistry lab let visitors do safe experiments, like creating erupting volcanoes with baking soda and vinegar. My favorite part was the planetarium, where we watched a show about the solar system. The narrator explained how vast the universe is and showed us beautiful images of distant galaxies. We also visited the dinosaur hall, where a huge T-Rex skeleton stood in the center. The museum had many hands-on activities that made learning fun and memorable. I left the museum feeling inspired to learn more about science.

passages.title: The Power of Kindness
passages.text: Small acts of kindness can make a big difference in someone's day. Last week, I witnessed a beautiful example of this. I was waiting for the bus when I saw an elderly woman struggling with heavy grocery bags. She looked tired and was having difficulty managing all the bags. A young man nearby noticed her situation. Without hesitation, he approached her and offered to help. "Let me carry those for you," he said with a warm smile. The woman's face lit up with gratitude. "Thank you so much, dear," she replied. The young man carried her bags to the bus stop and even helped her get on the bus. The entire interaction took only a few minutes, but the impact was tremendous. The woman seemed much happier, and other people at the bus stop were smiling too. It reminded me that kindness is contagious – when we see someone being kind, it inspires us to be kind as well.

passages.title: The History of the Olympic Games
passages.text: The Olympic Games have a long and fascinating history that dates back to ancient Greece. The first ancient Olympics were held in Olympia, Greece, in 776 BC. These games were held every four years for nearly 12 centuries. The ancient Olympics were quite different from today's games. Only men could compete, and they often competed naked. The games included events like running, wrestling, boxing, and chariot racing. The Olympics were so important that all wars stopped during the games, a period called the "Olympic Truce." The ancient Olympics ended around 400 AD when the Roman Emperor banned them. The modern Olympics began in 1896 when a French educator named Pierre de Coubertin revived the games. He believed that sports could promote peace and understanding between nations. Today, the Olympics include both Summer and Winter games, and athletes from all over the world participate.

passages.title: My First Job Interview
passages.text: Last month, I applied for a part-time job at a local bookstore. When they called me for an interview, I was both excited and nervous. I had never been to a job interview before. My mom helped me prepare by practicing common interview questions. She told me to dress professionally and arrive early. On the day of the interview, I put on my best shirt and pants and arrived 15 minutes early. The manager, Ms. Lee, was very friendly. She asked me about my experience with books and why I wanted to work there. I explained that I love reading and that I thought working in a bookstore would be perfect for me. She also asked about my availability and whether I could work weekends. The interview lasted about 20 minutes. At the end, Ms. Lee said she would call me in a few days with her decision. Three days later, I got the call – I got the job! I was thrilled and couldn't wait to start working.

passages.title: The Mystery of Sleep
passages.text: We spend about one-third of our lives sleeping, yet sleep remains one of the greatest mysteries of human existence. While we sleep, our bodies may look inactive, but our brains are actually very busy. Sleep occurs in different stages, each serving important functions. During deep sleep, our bodies repair tissues and strengthen our immune system. During REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep, our brains process memories and emotions. Scientists have discovered that lack of sleep can have serious consequences. People who don't get enough sleep have trouble concentrating, making decisions, and controlling their emotions. Chronic sleep deprivation can even lead to health problems like obesity, diabetes, and heart disease. Different people need different amounts of sleep, but most adults need 7-9 hours per night. Creating a good sleep routine, such as going to bed at the same time each night and avoiding screens before bedtime, can help improve sleep quality.

passages.title: The Story of Chocolate Chip Cookies
passages.text: One of America's favorite treats, the chocolate chip cookie, was actually invented by accident. In 1938, Ruth Wakefield was running the Toll House Inn in Massachusetts. She was preparing chocolate cookies for her guests when she realized she was out of baker's chocolate. Instead, she broke up a bar of semi-sweet chocolate and added the pieces to her cookie dough, expecting them to melt and create chocolate cookies. However, the chocolate pieces held their shape, creating the world's first chocolate chip cookies. The guests loved them so much that Ruth began serving them regularly. Word spread about these amazing cookies, and soon people were asking for the recipe. Ruth eventually sold the recipe to Nestlé chocolate company in exchange for a lifetime supply of chocolate. The Toll House Chocolate Chip Cookie became so popular that it is now considered an American classic. Today, billions of chocolate chip cookies are eaten around the world every year.

passages.title: The Magic of Libraries
passages.text: Libraries are much more than just buildings filled with books. They are gateways to knowledge, imagination, and community. In ancient times, libraries were only for the wealthy and scholars. The famous Library of Alexandria in Egypt was one of the largest libraries in the ancient world. Today, public libraries serve everyone in the community. Modern libraries offer much more than books. You can borrow DVDs, use computers, attend workshops, and participate in reading programs. Many libraries have makerspaces where people can use 3D printers, learn coding, or work on craft projects. Libraries also serve as community centers where people can meet, study, and attend cultural events. For many people, libraries provide free access to information and technology that they might not otherwise have. The librarians are trained to help you find exactly what you need, whether it's a specific book, research information, or guidance on using technology. In our digital age, libraries continue to evolve and remain vital community resources.

passages.title: Learning a Foreign Language
passages.text: Learning a foreign language is one of the most rewarding challenges you can take on. When I started learning Spanish two years ago, I thought it would be impossible. The grammar seemed complicated, and the pronunciation was difficult. However, my teacher gave me some helpful advice. She said, "Don't be afraid to make mistakes. Mistakes are how we learn." She encouraged me to practice speaking with classmates, even if I wasn't perfect. I also started watching Spanish movies with subtitles and listening to Spanish music. Gradually, I began to understand more and more. Now, I can have simple conversations in Spanish, and I'm planning to visit Mexico next summer to practice with native speakers. Learning Spanish has opened up a new world for me. I can communicate with millions more people, understand different cultures better, and it even looks good on college applications. Most importantly, it has given me confidence that I can learn anything if I put my mind to it.

passages.title: The End of Summer
passages.text: As I sit by my bedroom window watching the leaves begin to change colors, I realize that summer is coming to an end. It feels like just yesterday that school finished and the long, sunny days of vacation began. This summer was filled with so many memorable experiences. I went camping with my family, learned to swim, read five novels, and made new friends at summer camp. I also accomplished something I never thought I could do – I learned to ride a bicycle without training wheels. My dad spent hours running alongside me in the park until I finally got the hang of it. Now, as September approaches, I have mixed feelings. I'm sad to see the warm weather and freedom of summer go, but I'm also excited about starting a new school year. I'll be in a new grade with new teachers and new subjects to learn. I'm curious about what challenges and adventures await me. Summer taught me that time passes quickly, so it's important to appreciate every moment and try new things. I'm grateful for all the wonderful memories I made.
